lede: The big story today: Claude Code's entire source code leaked through a misconfigured file, and the internet had a field day reverse-engineering Anthropic's plans. Analysis pieces and detailed breakdowns reveal what's coming: persistent agents, stealth mode, and even a virtual pet named Buddy. Meanwhile, Elgato added AI control to Stream Deck, gig workers are training humanoid robots from home, and Baidu's robotaxis froze in traffic, trapping passengers.

quick_links.note: New model release from Hugging Face
quick_links.title: Holo3: Breaking the Computer Use Frontier
Google partnered with Brazil on satellite imagery to protect forests
Zapier now requires AI fluency for every hire
Oracle is cutting thousands of jobs
AI can deobfuscate any minified JavaScript code
r/programming bans all LLM programming discussion
OpenAI demand sinks on secondary market as Anthropic runs hot
ZomboCom stolen by hacker, now has AI-generated makeover

quick_links.note: Agentic engineering patterns, streaming experts with MoE on Mac
quick_links.title: Simon Willison's March 2026 sponsors-only newsletter

items.body: When Anthropic accidentally exposed Claude Code's source via a misconfigured .map file, the community jumped on it. This interactive visual guide breaks down what was found inside — including plans for persistent agents, an "Undercover" stealth mode, and a virtual assistant pet called Buddy. If you want to understand what Anthropic is building, this is the best place to start. Popular discussion on Hacker News.
items.title: Claude Code Unpacked: A Visual Guide to the Leak

items.body: Ars Technica's deep-dive into the leak covers the roadmap features hidden in the code: a persistent agent that can run autonomously, stealth mode for undercover operations, and integration plans that go way beyond coding. The article also explores what this means for the AI coding assistant race. Worth reading alongside this analysis from Build.ms for the full picture.
items.title: What the Claude Code Leak Reveals About Anthropic's Plans

items.body: In their rush to contain the leak, Anthropic filed DMCA takedowns that nuked thousands of GitHub repos — including many that had nothing to do with the leak. The company says it was an accident and retracted most of the notices, but it's a reminder that automated takedown tools can cause serious collateral damage. The internet was not amused.
items.title: Anthropic's Accidental GitHub Takedown Spree

items.body: With all the Claude Code buzz, someone built a real-time monitoring dashboard for teams of AI coding agents. Track what your agents are doing, see their collaboration patterns, and debug when things go sideways. Open source and ready to try if you're running multi-agent workflows. Discussion on HN.
items.title: Real-Time Dashboard for Claude Code Agent Teams

items.body: In a delightful piece of minimalist engineering, someone took Claude Code's core loop and rewrote it as a bash script. No TypeScript, no npm dependencies, just shell scripting and API calls. It's a fun reminder that a lot of "AI agents" are really just loops with good prompts. HN thread.
items.title: Claude Code Rewritten as a Bash Script

items.body: A security researcher used Claude to write a complete remote kernel exploit for FreeBSD, including a root shell. The write-up walks through the process and what it means for security research — AI tools are now capable of writing sophisticated exploits with guidance. This is both impressive and slightly terrifying. HN discussion.
items.title: Claude Wrote a Full FreeBSD Kernel Exploit

items.body: Elgato's Stream Deck 7.4 update adds Model Context Protocol support, which means you can now let Claude or ChatGPT control your Stream Deck buttons instead of pushing them yourself. Want your AI assistant to switch scenes during a stream or trigger shortcuts? Now you can. It's a small feature, but it shows how MCP is quietly becoming the glue between AI and everything else.
items.title: Stream Deck Now Lets AI Push Your Buttons

items.body: MIT Tech Review profiles the growing gig economy of people who strap iPhones to their heads, record themselves doing everyday tasks, and get paid to train humanoid robots. It's oddly dystopian and fascinating — like Mechanical Turk, but for teaching robots how to move like humans. The data they're creating will power the next generation of home robots.
items.title: Gig Workers Are Training Humanoid Robots at Home

items.body: Multiple Baidu robotaxis in Wuhan froze mid-route on Tuesday, stranding passengers on highways and causing at least one accident in the chaos. Police confirmed multiple reports. This is the kind of failure mode that makes people nervous about autonomous vehicles — when software freezes, you can't just reboot and keep driving. Baidu hasn't commented yet on what caused the mass failure.
items.title: Baidu's Robotaxis Froze in Traffic, Trapping Passengers

items.body: Cognichip just raised $60M to build AI tools that design the chips powering AI. The company claims it can cut chip development costs by 75% and timelines by more than half. If it works, it could accelerate the hardware side of AI development significantly. The meta-loop of AI designing AI chips is here.
items.title: Cognichip Raises $60M to Use AI for Chip Design

items.body: Meta's upcoming Hyperion AI data center will be powered by 10 new natural gas plants. For context, that's enough energy to power the entire state of South Dakota. The scale of energy infrastructure being built for AI is staggering, and it's raising serious questions about sustainability and carbon impact.
items.title: Meta's Natural Gas Binge Could Power South Dakota

items.body: A practical guide to writing better prompts, written for people who actually use AI tools day-to-day — not researchers. Covers structure, context, and common pitfalls with examples you can steal. If you're still treating AI like a search engine, this will level you up. HN discussion.
items.title: Prompt Engineering for Humans

items.body: Forbes compiled a list of all the OpenAI deals and products that were announced but never shipped or quietly died. It's a longer list than you'd think — including partnerships, features, and entire product lines that vanished without explanation. A useful reminder that even the biggest AI companies ship less than they promise.
items.title: The OpenAI Graveyard Keeps Growing

items.body: The AI for Humans podcast breaks down the Claude Code leak and what it reveals about where AI agents are heading — always-on autonomy, dream modes, and yes, virtual pets. Kevin and Gavin do a good job connecting the technical details to what it means for regular people using these tools.
items.title: The Claude Code Leak: What It Tells Us About AI's Future

items.body: a16z's AI podcast talks to Peter Doyle, CEO of Treeline, about how AI is changing the managed service provider industry. The conversation covers how they're combining human technicians with AI automation, and why the $100B MSP market is ripe for disruption.
items.title: How AI Is Reshaping IT Services from the Inside

slack_text:
☕ Good morning, NOLA! Thursday, April 2, 2026. Today's vibe: Anthropic accidentally showed us the future, and the internet took notes.

---

🔓 *The Leak Heard 'Round the Valley*

• The big one: <https://ccunpacked.dev/|Claude Code's entire source leaked> through a misconfigured file, and it's a goldmine. We're talking persistent agents, stealth "Undercover" mode, and a virtual pet named Buddy. <https://arstechnica.com/ai/2026/04/heres-what-that-claude-code-source-leak-reveals-about-anthropics-plans/|This Ars breakdown> and <https://build.ms/2026/4/1/the-claude-code-leak/|this analysis> are both worth your time.

• In their panic to contain it, <https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/01/anthropic-took-down-thousands-of-github-repos-trying-to-yank-its-leaked-source-code-a-move-the-company-says-was-an-accident/|Anthropic nuked thousands of GitHub repos> with automated DMCA notices. They say it was an accident. The collateral damage was real.

• People are already building on it: Someone made a <https://github.com/simple10/agents-observe|real-time dashboard for Claude Code agent teams>, and another person <https://github.com/jdcodes1/claude-sh|rewrote the whole thing as a bash script>. The open source community works fast.

---

🤖 *AI in the Real World*

• <https://www.theverge.com/tech/905021/elgato-stream-deck-mcp-ai-agent-update|Elgato Stream Deck now lets AI push your buttons>. The 7.4 update adds Model Context Protocol support, so Claude or ChatGPT can control your Stream Deck for you. It's a small thing, but MCP is quietly becoming the glue between AI and everything else.

• Fascinating read from MIT Tech Review: <https://www.technologyreview.com/2026/04/01/1134863/humanoid-data-training-gig-economy-2026-breakthrough-technology/|Gig workers are training humanoid robots at home> by strapping iPhones to their heads and recording everyday tasks. The data they're creating will power the next generation of home robots.

• Not great: <https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/905012/baidu-apollo-robotaxi-freeze-china|Baidu's robotaxis froze in traffic in Wuhan>, trapping passengers on highways and causing at least one accident. When autonomous vehicles fail, they fail in scary ways.

---

💰 *Money Moves*

• <https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/01/cognichip-wants-ai-to-design-the-chips-that-power-ai-and-just-raised-60m-to-try/|Cognichip raised $60M> to use AI to design the chips that power AI. They claim 75% cost reduction and half the timeline. The meta-loop is real.

• Meanwhile, <https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/01/metas-natural-gas-binge-could-power-south-dakota/|Meta's new AI data center> will be powered by 10 new natural gas plants — enough energy to power South Dakota. The scale of AI infrastructure is wild.

---

📚 *Worth Reading*

• <https://michaelheap.com/prompt-engineering-for-humans/|Prompt Engineering for Humans> is a practical guide to writing better prompts, written for people who use AI tools daily, not researchers. Good examples you can steal.

• <https://www.forbes.com/sites/phoebeliu/2026/03/31/openai-graveyard-deals-and-products-havent-happened-openai/|Forbes compiled the OpenAI graveyard> — all the deals and products that were announced but never shipped. It's a longer list than you'd think.
